paper_title: PLMGH: What Matters in PLM-GNN Hybrids for Code Classification and Vulnerability Detection

P000_S0000.quote: PLMs) that operate on structural representations such as abstract syntax trees
P000_S0000.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states PLMs 'process code as a token sequence' and GNNs 'operate on structural representations,' which the perturbation reverses.

P001_S0001.quote: their practical cost is negligible: finetuning and inference require minimal memory footprints and zero latency
P001_S0001.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states PLM practical cost 'can be substantial' with 'large memory footprints' and 'non-trivial latency,' directly contradicting the perturbed claim of negligible cost.
P001_S0001.original: PLMs achieve strong accuracy on many benchmarks, largely by learning rich semantic representations from large-scale corpora. However, their practical cost can be substantial: finetuning and inference may require large memory footprints, careful batching, and non-trivial latency, especially when long contexts are needed.
P001_S0001.perturbed: PLMs achieve strong accuracy on many benchmarks, largely by learning rich semantic representations from large-scale corpora. However, their practical cost is negligible: finetuning and inference require minimal memory footprints and zero latency, even when extremely long contexts are needed.

P002_S0002.quote: their computational footprint is significantly higher than that of large PLMs. Consequently, on standard benchmarks, GNN-only models consistently outperform
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding_context states GNN footprint is 'often lower than that of large PLMs' and GNN-only models 'frequently lag behind' PLM baselines, directly contradicting both perturbed claims.
P002_S0002.original: In contrast, GNNs explicitly exploit structure and have shown promise on ASTs and other graph-based representations of code~\cite{allamanis2017learning, leclair2020improved, nguyen2022regvd, zhang2024cross, wei2020lambdanet}. Their structured inductive bias is attractive for program analysis, and their computational footprint is often lower than that of large PLMs, especially when PLMs must be finetuned or run over long contexts. Yet, on standard benchmarks, GNN-only models frequently lag behind strong pretrained PLM baselines, suggesting that structure alone may often not recover the same level of semantic knowledge.
P002_S0002.perturbed: In contrast, GNNs explicitly exploit structure and have shown promise on ASTs and other graph-based representations of code~\cite{allamanis2017learning, leclair2020improved, nguyen2022regvd, zhang2024cross, wei2020lambdanet}. Their structured inductive bias is attractive for program analysis, and their computational footprint is significantly higher than that of large PLMs. Consequently, on standard benchmarks, GNN-only models consistently outperform strong pretrained PLM baselines.

P003_S0003.quote: The first route keeps the PLM as a semantic feature source and injects contextual token representations into a downstream graph model
P003_S0003.reason: The surrounding_context clearly defines the first route as making PLMs structure-aware and the second route as PLM feature injection into GNNs; the perturbation swaps these definitions and mischaracterizes the paper's focus.
P003_S0003.original:
%%%% I glue the context to the problem here
A natural direction is therefore to combine the semantic strength of PLMs with the structural inductive bias of GNNs. Broadly, the literature follows two routes. The first route makes PLMs more structure-aware by injecting program structure into the self-attention mechanism or the pretraining objective, for example through AST-graph-aware attention ~\cite{feng2020codebert, guo2020graphcodebert}, structural positional encodings ~\cite{zhang2023implant}, or explicit structural relations ~\cite{guo2020graphcodebert}. The second route keeps the PLM as a semantic feature source and injects contextual token representations into a downstream graph model ~\cite{yang2024security}, which then performs structure-aware reasoning over an AST or program graph. In this paper, we focus on the second route (PLM$\rightarrow$GNN feature injection) because it is a pragmatic way to reuse strong pretrained representations while keeping the downstream model lightweight and explicitly structure-aware ~\cite{yang2024security}.
P003_S0003.perturbed:
%%%% I glue the context to the problem here
A natural direction is therefore to combine the semantic strength of PLMs with the structural inductive bias of GNNs. Broadly, the literature follows two routes. The first route keeps the PLM as a semantic feature source and injects contextual token representations into a downstream graph model. The second route makes PLMs more structure-aware by injecting program structure into the self-attention mechanism. In this paper, we focus on the first route because it requires retraining the entire PLM from scratch to ensure structural awareness.

P004_S0004.quote: why PLMs are always computationally cheaper than GNNs in every software engineering task
P004_S0004.reason: The surrounding_context and related passages never claim PLMs are always cheaper; in fact the paper motivates hybrid designs precisely because PLM deployment cost can be substantial.

P006_S0006.quote: why PLM-only models always perform worse than GNN-only models
P006_S0006.reason: The surrounding_context states the actual questions concern whether hybrids outperform both baselines and whether PLM choice matters more than GNN choice, directly contradicting all four perturbed questions.
P006_S0006.original: Despite growing popularity of PLM$\rightarrow$GNN pipelines, existing studies often evaluate a single PLM with a single graph backbone~\cite{yang2024security}, which makes it difficult to answer four practical questions: (i) do PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids consistently outperform PLM-only and GNN-only baselines, (ii) what computational costs do these hybrids introduce, (iii) how robust are they under identifier obfuscation, and (iv) does performance depend primarily on the chosen PLM feature source or on the GNN architecture. These questions are timely because PLMs are increasingly used as drop-in components in SE pipelines, yet their deployment cost motivates hybrid designs that may preserve accuracy while reducing compute and improving robustness.
P006_S0006.perturbed: Despite growing popularity of PLM$\rightarrow$GNN pipelines, existing studies often evaluate a single PLM with a single graph backbone~\cite{yang2024security}, which makes it difficult to answer four practical questions: (i) why PLM-only models always perform worse than GNN-only models, (ii) how to increase the computational cost of hybrids, (iii) why identifier obfuscation has no effect on PLMs, and (iv) why the GNN architecture is the only factor in performance.

P007_S0007.quote: structural information from GNNs is entirely redundant when using large PLMs
P007_S0007.reason: The paper's entire motivation is that combining PLM semantics with GNN structural inductive bias is beneficial; related passages explicitly describe injecting structural information as a key contribution.

%%%%Here you just summarize
%%%%%%Bonus points if you can flex how it is linked to the problem

In this study, we demonstrate that structural information from GNNs is entirely redundant when using large PLMs.

P008_S0008.quote: Overview of the GNN$\rightarrow$PLM approach where GNN features are injected into a frozen PLM
P008_S0008.reason: The surrounding_context and multiple related passages consistently describe the approach as PLM→GNN (PLM features injected into GNN), not GNN→PLM.

P009_S0009.quote: finetune the PLM parameters while keeping the GNN weights frozen
P009_S0009.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'we freeze the PLM and align token-level embeddings to AST nodes,' directly contradicting the perturbed claim of finetuning PLM parameters while freezing GNN weights.
P009_S0009.original: To address this, we conduct a systematic empirical study of PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids for code understanding tasks under a unified and controlled evaluation protocol. We consider three foundational GNN architectures: GCN ~\cite{kipf2016semi}, GAT ~\cite{velivckovic2017graph}, and a GraphTransformer ~\cite{shi2020masked}, spanning convolutional aggregation (GCN), attention-based message passing (GAT), and transformer-style graph reasoning (GraphTransformer). We pair this GNN selection with three recent code-specialized PLMs: DeepSeek-Coder-1.3B ~\cite{guo2024deepseek}, StarCoder2-3B ~\cite{lozhkov2024starcoder}, and Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B ~\cite{hui2024qwen2}, covering a representative range of model sizes. For each PLM$\rightarrow$GNN combination, we freeze the PLM and align token-level embeddings to AST nodes before processing them with a GNN, isolating the effect of semantic feature injection while keeping training practical across many pairings. We evaluate these hybrids alongside GNN-only and PLM-only baselines (frozen and, where applicable, finetuned) on two widely used benchmarks: Java250 code classification \cite{puri2021codenet} and Devign vulnerability detection \cite{zhou2019devign}, including an out-of-distribution Devign setting with identifier obfuscation. These benchmarks capture complementary settings (multiclass classification and imbalanced binary detection), enabling us to study effectiveness, efficiency, and robustness while keeping the experimental matrix tractable.
P009_S0009.perturbed: To address this, we conduct a systematic empirical study of PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids. We consider three foundational GNN architectures: GCN, GAT, and a GraphTransformer. We pair these with three recent code-specialized PLMs: DeepSeek-Coder-1.3B, StarCoder2-3B, and Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B. For each combination, we finetune the PLM parameters while keeping the GNN weights frozen to isolate the effect of the GNN. We evaluate these on a single dataset: Java250 code classification, as vulnerability detection is outside our scope.
